stories.headline: Signal No. 1 raised over northeastern Cagayan as Super Typhoon Inday nears PAR entry
stories.summary: PAGASA raised Tropical Cyclone Wind Signal No. 1 over 12 areas of Northern Luzon on Wednesday, July 8, including Batanes, Cagayan, Isabela, Apayao, Kalinga and Ilocos Norte, as Super Typhoon Inday (international name Bavi) moved over the Philippine Sea east of Luzon. In its 5 p.m. bulletin PAGASA reported maximum sustained winds of 195 kph and gusts to 240 kph, with the storm moving generally westward at about 20 kph, and said Inday would keep super typhoon strength through Thursday before gradually weakening.
stories.context: President Marcos ordered a whole-of-government response and urged public vigilance. Disaster agencies said the DSWD pre-positioned about 4.7 million family food packs, the DOH declared a Code White alert, and the MMDA readied its 73 pumping stations. PAGASA and the Office of Civil Defense warned that the enhanced southwest monsoon could bring flooding and rain-induced landslides beyond the storm's track, with heaviest rains expected Friday to Sunday.
stories.watch_for: PAGASA said the storm is forecast to pass nearest Extreme Northern Luzon around July 10 to 11 and to exit the Philippine Area of Responsibility by about July 11 on a track toward East Asia; it said the highest signal that may be raised during Inday's passage is Signal No. 2 or 3. Gale warnings remained over the eastern seaboards of Northern and Central Luzon and the Visayas.

stories.headline: DOE issues 203 show-cause orders to power firms over missing reliability reports
stories.summary: The Department of Energy issued 203 show-cause orders to power generation companies nationwide, 174 to on-grid plants and 29 to off-grid facilities, for failing to submit mandatory annual self-assessment reports on plant reliability and outages. The reports were originally due March 31 under Department Circular 2026-02-0006, a deadline the DOE later extended to April 24.
stories.context: DOE legal officer Gabriel Corpuz said the orders were issued solely for failing to submit the required paperwork on time and do not automatically mean a plant was responsible for forced outages or capacity problems. Firms found in violation face possible warnings, fines, or suspension or revocation of their certificates of compliance.

stories.headline: LandBank scraps InstaPay and PESONet transfer fees
stories.summary: State-owned Land Bank of the Philippines eliminated fees on InstaPay and PESONet fund transfers for retail clients starting Tuesday, July 7, extending an earlier waiver on QRPh person-to-government payments to everyday retail and business transfers. The waiver also covers Overseas Filipino Bank clients using LandBank's mobile app and iAccess platform.
stories.context: LandBank joins BPI and RCBC, which dropped interbank transfer fees on July 1 and July 4 respectively, following a Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas circular effective July 4 that limits interbank transfer fees to the actual switching cost, estimated at about P1.50.

stories.headline: Agriculture chief pushes to extend P50-per-kilo cap on imported rice
stories.summary: Agriculture Secretary Francisco Tiu Laurel Jr. cited June's cooler inflation reading, including rice inflation easing to 15.0 percent from 15.6 percent, as grounds for extending the government's P50-per-kilogram price ceiling on imported 5-percent broken rice for another 60 days. He said the cap has helped bring average retail prices down from about P51 to P52 per kilogram to roughly P47 to P48 during the current lean production season.
stories.context: The P50 price cap was imposed under Executive Order 118 in May to guard against unjustified price increases amid food-cost pressure linked to the Middle East conflict; officials have flagged concerns that retailers raised prices whenever the cap previously lapsed.

stories.headline: Peso strengthens as June inflation eases for second straight month
stories.summary: The peso gained 5.6 centavos to close at P61.435 against the US dollar on Tuesday, from P61.491 the prior session, as traders reacted to June's headline inflation easing to 6.4 percent and to falling global oil prices. Analysts said the currency could trade between P61.35 and P61.55 on Wednesday.
stories.context: The Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas has raised policy rates by a total of 50 basis points since April to keep inflation expectations anchored amid the oil-price shock caused by the Middle East conflict.

stories.headline: Marcos presides over AFP command conference in Brawner's final turnover before retirement
stories.summary: President Marcos attended the Armed Forces of the Philippines' 1st Semester 2026 Command Conference at Camp Aguinaldo on July 7, where military officials briefed him on operational accomplishments and security priorities for the coming months. It was the last such conference to be led by outgoing AFP Chief of Staff Gen. Romeo Brawner Jr. ahead of his retirement.
stories.context: Defense Secretary Gilberto Teodoro Jr. and senior AFP leaders nationwide also attended. Malacañang did not disclose further specifics of the operational report presented to Marcos.

stories.headline: Government approves 2024 performance-based bonus for public employees
stories.summary: Malacañang approved the release of the fiscal year 2024 Performance-Based Bonus for qualified national government personnel, state university and college staff, government-owned firms, water districts and local government units, the Department of Budget and Management said. Congress, the judiciary, constitutional commissions and the Ombudsman are also covered.
stories.context: To qualify, agencies must score at least 70 points across performance, process, financial and citizen-satisfaction measures, and employees must have served at least nine months in 2024 with a very satisfactory rating; those found guilty of administrative or criminal cases by final judgment in that year are disqualified.

stories.headline: Second landslide in two days shuts Bukidnon highway
stories.summary: A landslide before dawn on Monday, July 6, buried about 40 to 45 meters of the San Fernando-Talaingod Road in Sitio Balakayo, Barangay Kalagangan, San Fernando, Bukidnon, after days of heavy rain, local disaster officials said. No casualties were reported, but the closure forced motorists to detour through the Bukidnon-Davao road or Barangay Matupe.
stories.context: It was the second landslide to hit the same road corridor in as many days, after an earlier slide at Sitio Cabadiangan, Talaingod, on July 5 that had just been cleared. Officials could not yet estimate when the newly blocked stretch would reopen.

stories.headline: UN warns of 'red alert' as fighting escalates around Sudan's el-Obeid
stories.summary: More than 11,000 people, over half of them children, have fled el-Obeid in Sudan over the past two weeks as Rapid Support Forces intensified pressure on the army-held city while the Sudanese Armed Forces reinforced positions, according to Save the Children and the United Nations. The UN human rights chief, Volker Turk, called the situation a red alert, warning that up to 500,000 civilians could be at risk if the violence escalates further.
stories.context: El-Obeid is a strategic commercial and logistics hub about 360 kilometers southwest of Khartoum. Officials fear it could face a siege similar to el-Fasher's, where prolonged urban warfare devastated services and trapped civilians; damage to water infrastructure has also raised cholera concerns.

stories.headline: Taiwan VP links assault on Japanese journalist to China's new unity law
stories.summary: A Japanese journalist based in Taiwan was punched in the face after giving a speech on the island, and a Hong Kong man was arrested in connection with the attack. Taiwanese Vice President Hsiao Bi-khim said the assault, on a journalist she described as a critic of Beijing, may be connected to a new Chinese law promoting ethnic unity.
stories.context: The incident has raised concern about the extraterritorial reach of China's ethnic unity legislation and its potential effect on press freedom and foreign nationals critical of Beijing operating outside mainland China.

stories.headline: Duterte impeachment trial enters defense cross-examination on Day 3
stories.summary: The impeachment trial of Vice President Sara Duterte moved into its third day on Wednesday, July 8, with her defense team cross-examining the prosecution's first witness, National Bureau of Investigation agent John Mark Calilung, who authenticated a video of Duterte's November 2024 online press conference. Defense counsel argued the recorded remarks were not an impeachable offense, saying there was no proof she contracted an assassin and that she spoke as a family member reacting to an alleged plot against her relatives.
stories.context: Article IV of the complaint accuses Duterte of a grave threat over remarks in the November 2024 briefing that she had arranged to have President Marcos, First Lady Liza Araneta-Marcos and then-Speaker Martin Romualdez killed if she were harmed. The Senate, sitting as an impeachment court under presiding officer Francis Escudero, began the trial proper this week.
stories.watch_for: The court ordered Duterte's former chief of staff, lawyer Zuleika Lopez, to appear as a witness on Tuesday, July 14. The defense is allotted 30 trial days to present its case following the prosecution's phase.

stories.headline: Group asks Supreme Court to halt Duterte impeachment trial over leadership dispute
stories.summary: A group asked the Supreme Court to stop the Senate impeachment court from proceeding with Vice President Sara Duterte's trial, filing an urgent motion that challenges a June 3 Senate rule change allowing senators to elect a presiding officer in non-presidential impeachment cases. The petitioners sought a temporary restraining order or similar relief, arguing the trial began under disputed leadership, and said they were not asking the Court to rule on Duterte's guilt or innocence.
stories.context: Senator Francis Escudero was elected to preside over the trial this week after a contested Senate reorganization. Critics including Senator Alan Peter Cayetano have argued the rule amendment was adopted without a quorum and without the required advance notice.

stories.headline: Manila 'notes with concern' Chinese missile test over the Pacific
stories.summary: The Department of Foreign Affairs said on Wednesday, July 8, that it noted with concern a Chinese missile launch and had been given notice a day before the July 6 test. A day earlier the Department of National Defense called the launch a provocative display of military power. China's navy fired the missile, which carried a dummy warhead, from the South China Sea; a flight-path map presented by Taiwan showed it crossing northern Luzon before landing in the Pacific, according to Philippine Star.
stories.context: New Zealand, Australia and the Solomon Islands also criticized the test, and the US Embassy in Manila said it shared the Philippines' concerns. The launch adds to tensions between Manila and Beijing over the South China Sea.

stories.headline: Defense chief says transfer of five Japanese destroyers to Philippines is finalized
stories.summary: Defense Secretary Gilberto Teodoro Jr. said the transfer of five Abukuma-class destroyer escorts from Japan to the Philippines is settled, with only administrative details remaining, according to Philippine Star citing the Philippine News Agency. The vessels are expected to be delivered within two to three years. If completed, the deal would be Japan's first overseas transfer of retired surface warships from its Maritime Self-Defense Force under its defense equipment transfer framework.
stories.context: The Abukuma class is a group of six escort ships commissioned between 1989 and 1993, each about 2,000 tons. Manila and Tokyo formed a working group on the transfer after Teodoro met his Japanese counterpart earlier this year, part of deepening defense ties amid South China Sea tensions.
stories.watch_for: Teodoro said the Philippine military would assess the vessels' condition and consider additional berthing and docking facilities before their arrival.

stories.headline: Philippine unemployment rises to 2.5 million in May, jobless rate at 4.8%
stories.summary: The Philippine Statistics Authority reported on Wednesday, July 8, that about 2.5 million Filipinos were unemployed in May, up from 2.03 million a year earlier, lifting the jobless rate to 4.8 percent from 3.9 percent in May 2025. National Statistician Claire Dennis Mapa said the biggest losses were in agriculture and forestry, which shed roughly 905,000 workers year on year, which Manila Bulletin linked to El Niño farm damage. About 49.63 million Filipinos were employed.
stories.context: The survey covers May 2026, when headline inflation stood at 6.8 percent before easing to 6.4 percent in June. Labor force participation was 63.8 percent. The PSA said it had not yet assessed the effect of a recently announced wage increase in Metro Manila.

stories.headline: Visayas power grid placed on red alert amid supply shortfall
stories.summary: The National Grid Corporation of the Philippines said it raised a red alert over the Visayas grid on Wednesday, July 8, from 3 p.m. to 9 p.m., warning of possible power interruptions or rotating brownouts. NGCP said available capacity of 2,397 megawatts fell short of a peak demand of 2,492 megawatts, and it attributed the shortfall to a tripped transmission line and the emergency shutdown of a power plant, with dozens of other plants offline or running at reduced capacity.
stories.context: A grid is placed on red alert when available power supply is insufficient to cover demand plus required reserves, raising the risk of outages. NGCP said 45 plants were on forced outage and 14 were running at reduced capacity, removing about 1,092 megawatts of capacity.

stories.headline: Trump says US-Iran ceasefire is 'over' after new exchange of strikes
stories.summary: US President Donald Trump said the ceasefire with Iran was over after the two sides traded strikes, according to BBC News. Iran said it had targeted US military sites in Bahrain and Kuwait in retaliation for American strikes, while Washington said it had revoked a temporary suspension of sanctions on Iranian oil sales. Speaking before a NATO summit in Turkey, Trump used disparaging language toward Iran's leadership and said further negotiations would be a waste of time. Oil prices rose after his remarks.
stories.context: The ceasefire had followed an earlier round of US and Israeli strikes on Iran. Iran's parliamentary speaker accused Washington of breaching a memorandum of understanding between the two sides. NATO's secretary general said the US strikes were necessary and accused Iran of violating the ceasefire.

stories.headline: Typhoon Maysak leaves at least 17 dead in southern China
stories.summary: At least 17 people have died, hundreds have been injured and tens of thousands have been evacuated after Typhoon Maysak brought floods and storms to southern China over the weekend, BBC News reported, citing Chinese state media. In Guangxi province, swollen rivers and breached dam walls inundated the city of Nanning and nearby villages, with residents seeking rescue from rooftops. The storm also spawned thunderstorms and rare tornadoes in Hubei. President Xi Jinping ordered all-out rescue operations.
stories.context: Chinese officials estimated about 60,000 people had been evacuated and at least 90,000 affected. Maysak was the first typhoon to make landfall in China this season; forecasters said Super Typhoon Bavi, the storm known in the Philippines as Inday, was expected to approach China's eastern coast later in the week.

stories.headline: Le Pen to seek French presidency despite upheld embezzlement conviction
stories.summary: French far-right leader Marine Le Pen said she would run in France's 2027 presidential election, hours after a Paris appeals court upheld her conviction for embezzling European Parliament funds, The Guardian reported. The court imposed a three-year sentence, two years suspended, with the remaining year under an electronic tag, plus a fine, and shortened her ban from office to a period she has already served, clearing her to run. Le Pen said she would appeal to France's highest court.
stories.context: The case centered on more than 2.8 million euros in European Parliament funds that prosecutors said were used to pay party staff between 2004 and 2016. Le Pen, who leads the National Rally, lost to President Emmanuel Macron in the 2017 and 2022 run-offs. Opposition figures criticized her candidacy.
